elements.sent-2411-002: Corresponding author: \tt{reinders@tnt.uni-hannover.de \footnotetext[1]{These authors contributed equally to this work.}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Current deep learning approaches in computer vision primarily focus on RGB data sacrificing information.
elements.sent-2411-004: In contrast, RAW images offer richer representation, which is crucial for precise recognition, particularly in challenging conditions like low-light environments.
elements.sent-2411-005: The resultant demand for comprehensive RAW image datasets contrasts with the labor-intensive process of creating specific datasets for individual sensors.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address this, we propose a novel diffusion-based method for generating RAW images guided by RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our approach integrates an RGB-guidance module for feature extraction from RGB inputs, then incorporates these features into the reverse diffusion process with RGB-guided residual blocks across various resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-008: This approach yields high-fidelity RAW images, enabling the creation of camera-specific RAW datasets.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our RGB2RAW experiments on four DSLR datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, RAW-Diffusion demonstrates exceptional data efficiency, achieving remarkable performance with as few as $25$ training samples or even fewer.
elements.sent-2411-011: We extend our method to create BDD100K-RAW and Cityscapes-RAW datasets, revealing its effectiveness for object detection in RAW imagery, significantly reducing the amount of required RAW images.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the traditional imaging pipeline, a camera sensor captures a RAW image, which is then converted via an Image Signal Processor (ISP) and compressed to a RGB image tailored for human consumption.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, while RGB images are convenient for human perception and readily available, their processing from RAW format introduces artifacts and information loss.
elements.sent-2411-015: Nonetheless, RGB images are ubiquitous in contemporary computer vision applications and serve as the primary data source for training neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-016: When running large computer vision models in the cloud, performing inference on RGB images is advantageous for reasons other than model performance: they are more cost-effective to transmit and store than their original RAW images.
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, when running CV models on the edge, under strict resource and hardware constraints, extracting the maximum information from the scene and avoiding unnecessary pre-processing in order to maximize small model performance becomes critical.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/Teaser_v1_small.png} \caption{ RAW-Diffusion enables the generation of high-fidelity RAW images by iterative denoising of a noisy RAW input through a RGB-guidance module and RGB-guided residual blocks. RAW-Diffusion is the first successful diffusion-based method for RAW generation that outperforms state-of-the-art methods. } \label{fig_teaser}
elements.sent-2411-018: The limitations of RGB images are particularly evident in scenarios requiring precise scene irradiance information, such as low-light environments or fine-grained image manipulation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-019: In contrast, RAW images captured directly by camera sensors offer a more faithful representation of scene data, retaining higher quantization levels and preserving the unprocessed sensor readings.
elements.sent-2411-020: The enhanced dynamic range of RAW images has demonstrated superior performance for a variety of image editing and computer vision tasks, including photometric stereo~\cite{shi2016benchmark , denoising~\cite{gharbi2016deep,abdelhamed2018high,brooks2019unprocessing , reflection removal~\cite{lei2023robust , low-light image enhancement~\cite{dagli2023diffuseraw , image super-resolution~\cite{zhang2019zoom , and object detection~\cite{otsukaSelfSupervisedReversedImage2023 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Employing RAW image inputs can thus improve the accuracy and robustness of neural networks across various computer vision tasks~\cite{buckler2017reconfiguring , especially when running on the edge.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, sensor-specific data collection and annotation for RAW datasets is extremely costly.
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, accessing RAW images can be challenging due to their memory-intensive nature, posing difficulties in storage, transfer, and sharing.
elements.sent-2411-024: Recent approaches aim to learn invertible functions for mapping between RGB and RAW images, achieving superior RAW synthesis performance.
elements.sent-2411-025: Among those, many methods~\cite{liang2021cameranet,liu2022deep,yoshimura2023dynamicisp mimic traditional ISP pipelines, requiring sensor-specific configurations but being more data efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Others~\cite{zamir2020cycleisp,xingInvertibleImageSignal2021,brooks2019unprocessing learn nonlinear mappings using RGB-RAW image pairs, often requiring many training samples.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Our goal is to develop a flexible, data-efficient, and high-performing method for RAW image synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this work, we present a novel diffusion model for RAW image reconstruction, employing features derived from an RGB image (see \cref{fig_teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike conventional methods that simply concatenate or add guidance signals \cite{liuInvertingImageSignal2022 , we introduce RGB-guided residual blocks for integrating RGB features and guiding the denoising of the RAW images.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This approach not only achieves state-of-the-art performance in RAW image reconstruction but also facilitates a data-efficient method for generating large-scale sensor-specific datasets using existing RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-031: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose a novel diffusion model for accurate RAW image reconstruction with RGB image guidance.
elements.sent-2411-033: The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in RAW image synthesis with high-bit sensors, especially for images featuring challenging irradiance distributions, and surpasses state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our method is extremely data-efficient, achieving the same performance with $25$ or fewer training samples as when training on the full training set.
elements.sent-2411-035: We enable the creation of camera-specific RAW datasets from existing RGB datasets, eliminating the need for costly sensor-specific data acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-036: The proposed method bridges the gap between models trained on RGB images for downstream tasks and RAW images, as shown for object detection.
elements.sent-2411-037: Unmapping the processed RGB images back to the RAW domain has been a longstanding research topic.
elements.sent-2411-038: Conventional radiometric calibration algorithms use multiple images taken with controlled exposures to compute the response function of a camera’s output intensity values with respect to the amount of light falling on the sensor~\cite{mitsunaga1999radiometric, grossberg2003determining,lin2005determining,debevec2023recovering , which are further advanced to handle saturated RGB values~\cite{chakrabarti2009empirical,kim2012new,chakrabarti2014modeling .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, these methods require calibration for a given camera, which involves multiple parameterized models for different camera settings.
elements.sent-2411-040: Modern deep learning-based algorithms~\cite{afifi2021cie,brooks2019unprocessing,conde2022model,condeReversedImageSignal2022,gharbi2016deep,schwartz2018deepisp,liu2022deep,zamir2020cycleisp,xingInvertibleImageSignal2021,liang2021cameranet,liu2022deep have shown how data-driven approaches can outperform conventional methods without the heavy need for calibration.}
elements.sent-2411-041: These methods either replace each or groups of ISP elements with neural networks~\cite{liang2021cameranet,liu2022deep,yoshimura2023dynamicisp or model RGB-to-RAW or RAW-to-RGB mapping with a single network~\cite{zamir2020cycleisp,xingInvertibleImageSignal2021,brooks2019unprocessing .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: While being more data efficient, the first approach requires sensor-specific configuration.
elements.sent-2411-043: In contrast, the single network approach requires large datasets of RGB-RAW image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-044: Diffusion models~\cite{hoDenoisingDiffusionProbabilistic2020 , a probabilistic generative approach, have recently gained prominence for their ability to produce high-quality images with diverse features.}
elements.sent-2411-045: They operate through a forward process, adding noise to clean samples, and a reverse process, recovering samples from noise~\cite{luo2022understanding,croitoru2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Offering stable training and strong priors compared to other generative models like GANs and VAEs~\cite{hoDenoisingDiffusionProbabilistic2020 , these models excel in various tasks like image generation~\cite{ho2022cascaded,dhariwal2021diffusion,nicholGLIDEPhotorealisticImage2022,rombach2022high,ramesh2022hierarchical , inpainting~\cite{lugmayr2022repaint,xie2023smartbrush , molecular generation~\cite{xuGeoDiffGeometricDiffusion2022 , audio synthesis~\cite{kongDiffWaveVersatileDiffusion2021 , and medical image analysis~\cite{kazerouniDiffusionModelsMedical2023 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs)~\cite{hoDenoisingDiffusionProbabilistic2020 , primarily focus on removing noise from images, while Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIMs)~\cite{songDenoisingDiffusionImplicit2021 generalize DDPMs in a more computationally efficient inference scheme via a non-Markovian forward process.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: As a generative model, they show the potential for enhancing datasets to improve downstream tasks like image classification~\cite{trabuccoEffectiveDataAugmentation2024 or object detection~\cite{fang2024data .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Deep learning methods have demonstrated remarkable performance across various domains of computer vision \cite{krizhevskyImageNetClassificationDeep2012,heDeepResidualLearning2016,zhangMixupEmpiricalRisk2018,dosovitskiyImageWorth16x162021,caoTrainingVisionTransformers2022,reindersChimeraMixImageClassification2022,heMaskedAutoencodersAre2022,reindersTwoWorldsOne2024,oquabDINOv2LearningRobust2023 , including significant improvements in object detection through data-driven approaches~\cite{zou2023object,carionEndendObjectDetection2020,redmon2016you,renFasterRCNNRealtime2015,reindersObjectRecognitionVery2018,reindersLearningConvolutionalNeural2019,liuGroundingDINOMarrying2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Object detection methods are generally classified into two categories: single-stage~\cite{sermanet2013overfeat,liuSSDSingleShot2016,lin2017focal,redmon2016you,tian2019fcos,tan2020efficientdet and two-stage methods approaches~\cite{girshick2014rich,lin2017feature,renFasterRCNNRealtime2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Notably, models like the YOLO~\cite{redmon2016you family and Faster R-CNN~\cite{renFasterRCNNRealtime2015 have been widely adopted as representative approaches.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recently, there has been a growing interest in leveraging RAW images for object detection~\cite{buckler2017reconfiguring,morawskiGenISPNeuralISP2022,yoshimura2023dynamicisp,yoshimura2023rawgment,ljungbergh2023raw,hong2021crafting , as they offer more precise information, particularly beneficial in low-light environments~\cite{hong2021crafting .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The proposed diffusion-based method is able to iteratively generate high-fidelity RAW images, simulate the distribution of the camera sensor, and create specific scenes by injecting RGB-guidance at multiple resolutions with RGB-guidance residual blocks into the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-054: In the following, we will briefly present the foundations of DDPMs.
elements.sent-2411-055: Afterward, the RGB-guidance module is introduced, which is followed by the architecture of the diffusion model including the conditional image generation.
elements.sent-2411-056: Finally, the training objective of the diffusion model is presented.
elements.sent-2411-057: Diffusion models \cite{sohl-dicksteinDeepUnsupervisedLearning2015,hoDenoisingDiffusionProbabilistic2020 are latent variable generative models inspired by non-equilibrium thermodynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the forward diffusion process, a Markov chain of $T$ steps is formulated by gradually adding random noise to the data.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given a data sample from the real data distribution $x_0 \sim q(x)$, a sequence of latent variables $x_1, \dots, x_T$ is generated by progressively adding Gaussian noise: \begin{equation}q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1}, \beta_tI),\end{equation} where $0 < \beta_t < 1$ is the variance schedule of the Gaussian noise, and $I$ the identity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-060: By defining $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha _t = \prod_{i=1 ^t \alpha_t$, $x_t$ can be sampled at an arbitrary time step in closed form using the reparametrization trick $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha _t x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha _t\epsilon$ with $\epsilon \in \mathcal{N (0, I)$, i.e., $q(x_t\mid x_0) = \mathcal{N (x_t; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha _t x_0, \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha _tI)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The reverse diffusion process is learned by training a parameterized model $p_{\theta}$ to recover the data and gradually remove noise.
elements.sent-2411-062: Starting from random noise $x_T \sim \mathcal{N (0, I)$, the distribution of $x_{t-1}$ given $x_{t}$ and $t$ is modeled as \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1};\mu_{\theta}(x_t, t), \sigma^2_tI)\end{equation} to generate new data samples, where $\mu_{\theta}$ is the Gaussian mean estimated by the denoising network and $\sigma_t$ is a fixed variance.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/Architecture_v2_small.png} \caption{The RAW-Diffusion architecture consists of an RGB-guidance module for creating guidance features and an encoder for processing noisy RAW inputs. The guidance features are then integrated into both the bottleneck and decoder with RGB-guidance residual blocks, modulating the diffusion features to reconstruct the RAW image. } \label{fig_overview}
elements.sent-2411-063: RAW-Diffusion is based on DDPMs and learns the reconstruction of a RAW image by starting from random noise and iterative denoising of a noisy RAW image guided by an RGB image.
elements.sent-2411-064: We introduce an RGB-guidance module that extracts features from the RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-065: The diffusion model, utilizing a U-Net architecture, injects the features into the diffusion process to guide the generation, and directly predicts the RAW image, without $\epsilon$-prediction.
elements.sent-2411-066: An overview of RAW-Diffusion is shown in \cref{fig_overview and the individual components are introduced in the following sections.}
elements.sent-2411-067: For high-fidelity RAW image synthesis, the guidance features from the ISP-processed RGB image $I_{\text{RGB \in \mathcal{R ^{3 \times H \times W}$ should contain both low- and high-level information.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Hence, we propose using the enhanced deep super-resolution network (EDSR)~\cite{limEnhancedDeepResidual2017 without an upsampling head for feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The RGB image $I_{\text{RGB }$ is first processed by a convolutional layer to lift the RGB channels to $C_{\text{GM,Features }$ channels, which are later fed to a sequence of $N_{\text{GM,ResBlocks }$ residual blocks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Each residual block consists of two convolutional layers followed by ReLU activation and a skip connection.
elements.sent-2411-071: The output of the residual blocks is then added to the initial convolved features, resulting in a feature map $F_{\text{RGB \in \mathcal{R ^{C \times H \times W}$, where $C=C_{\text{GM,Features }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: All convolutional layers employ $3 \times 3$ kernels with reflective padding of size 1, and the guidance module does not contain any normalization layers based on empirical results.
elements.sent-2411-073: The encoder network follows a standard architecture~\cite{dhariwalDiffusionModelsBeat2021 and derives a multi-scale feature representation of the noisy RAW image.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The input at each level is processed by $N_{\text{ResBlocks }$ residual blocks, followed by a downsampling layer that halves the spatial resolution, while expanding the feature dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Each residual block is characterized by two convolutional layers, each followed by SiLU activation ~\cite{hendrycksGaussianErrorLinear2016,elfwingSigmoidWeightedLinearUnits2018,ramachandranSearchingActivationFunctions2018 and group normalization \cite{wuGroupNormalization2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The current timestep $t$ of the diffusion process is mapped to a learnable embedding that is later projected onto a set of affine parameters for channel-specific transformation of the encoder features after the second group normalization.
elements.sent-2411-077: We also employ self-attention blocks after each residual block at specific resolutions to facilitate learning long-term dependencies between different spatial regions.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our objective is the guidance of the diffusion process for reconstructing RAW images using the relevant RGB information.
elements.sent-2411-079: For an overview of the fundamentals of guiding diffusion models, please refer to \cite{ho2021classifierfree,zhangAddingConditionalControl2023,mouT2IAdapterLearningAdapters2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-080: A naive solution involves concatenating the additional information directly with the noisy input.
elements.sent-2411-081: However, we introduce an RGB-guided residual block to inject RGB information into the network, enhancing the RAW image reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-082: For that, we integrate the RGB-guidance features by modulating the feature transformation within the residual block.
elements.sent-2411-083: The RGB-guided residual block is inspired by the semantic diffusion model \cite{wangSemanticImageSynthesis2022 which uses spatially-adaptive normalization (SPADE) \cite{parkSemanticImageSynthesis2019 to condition the diffusion process.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We introduce two layer-specific modules $\gamma^i$ and $\beta^i$ which downscale the RGB guidance features by bilinear interpolation and predict modulation parameters $\gamma^i(x) \in \mathcal{R ^{C \times H' \times W'}$ and $\beta^i(x) \in \mathcal{R ^{C \times H' \times W'}$ for each feature and spatial position, where $H' \times W'$ is the size of the feature map and $C$ number of channels.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The modules $\gamma^i$ and $\beta^i$ consist of a shared convolutional layer with ReLU activation followed by another individual convolutional layer.
elements.sent-2411-086: Afterward, the RGB information is integrated by scaling and shifting the normalized diffusion features $F^i$ as follows: \begin{equation}F^{i+1} = \operatorname{Norm}(F^{i}) \cdot (1 + \gamma^i(\operatorname{Down}(F_{\text{RGB}}))) + \beta^i(\operatorname{Down}(F_{\text{RGB}})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: The RGB-guided spatially adaptive normalization envelops both instances of group normalization within each residual block, learning to incorporate the relevant RGB information across multiple resolutions to guide the RAW image reconstruction process effectively.
elements.sent-2411-088: Starting with the bottleneck, we inject the RGB-guidance features into the diffusion process by modulating the high-level diffusion features with the proposed RGB-guided residual block.
elements.sent-2411-089: The bottleneck is composed of an RGB-guided ResBlock, an attention block, and another RGB-guided ResBlock.
elements.sent-2411-090: The decoder, symmetric to the encoder, takes the concatenation of the upscaled features from the preceding level and the shortcut connection from the encoder as input at each layer.
elements.sent-2411-091: Each decoder level is composed of a series of RGB-guided residual blocks and attention blocks.
elements.sent-2411-092: The final level decoder features are then projected via a convolutional layer and a hyperbolic tangent function to compose the RAW image.
elements.sent-2411-093: Our method directly predicts the denoised RAW image rather than estimating noise.
elements.sent-2411-094: The diffusion model is trained with pairs of RAW and RGB images, denoted as $I_{\text{RAW }$ and $I_{\text{RGB }$ In each training step, noisy RAW images and RGB images are provided as input, and the model is optimized to directly predict the RAW image $\hat{I _{\text{RAW }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We define three loss functions to facilitate the accurate reconstruction of the original RAW image.
elements.sent-2411-096: First, the squared difference and mean absolute difference are optimized via $\lossmse$ and $\lossLone$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-097: In addition, to address the bias in the distribution of the RAW pixel values towards low values, a logarithmic L1 loss \cite{eilertsenHDRImageReconstruction2017 , defined as $ \losslogLone(\hat{I , I) = \frac{1 {CHW \normfixed{\log(\hat{I + \epsilon) - \log(I+ \epsilon) _1$, where $\epsilon$ serves as a minimal constant to ensure numerical stability, is adopted.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: For the $\losslogLone$, the value range is adjusted to $[0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Hence, the overall training objective is defined as the composition of all three losses: $\mathcal{L = \mathcal{L _{\text{MSE + \mathcal{L _{\text{L1 + \mathcal{L _{\text{logL1 }$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: We perform extensive experiments to evaluate the performance of RAW-Diffusion and state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-101: The experiments are performed on diverse image sets captured by four different DSLR cameras, two from each the MIT-Adobe FiveK~\cite{bychkovskyLearningPhotographicGlobal2011 and NOD datasets~\cite{morawskiGenISPNeuralISP2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Following \cite{xingInvertibleImageSignal2021 , we use 777 and 590 images captured by the Canon EOS 5D camera and Nikon D700 cameras, respectively, and use a train-test split of $85/15$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The Night Object Detection (NOD) dataset \cite{morawskiGenISPNeuralISP2022 consists of RAW images recorded by Sony RX100 VII and Nikon D750, with object annotations of three classes.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The Sony camera has 3.2k images with 18.7k annotated instances, and Nikon has 4.0k with 28.0k annotated instances.
elements.sent-2411-105: We use the official training and test split.
elements.sent-2411-106: All RAW images are processed using the RawPy library to generate the corresponding RGB image.
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, we integrate two large-scale RGB datasets: Cityscapes \cite{cordtsCityscapesDatasetSemantic2016 and BDD100K \cite{Yu2018BDD100KAD (denoted as CS and BDD).}}

elements.sent-2411-109: BDD100K is a diverse dataset with 100k videos and various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-110: The dataset provides object detection annotations for 100k keyframes.

elements.sent-2411-217: In this work, we presented RAW-Diffusion, a novel diffusion-based method for generating high-fidelity RAW images guided by RGB images.
elements.sent-2411-218: RAW-Diffusion introduces an RGB-guidance module for extracting low-level and high-level guidance features.
elements.sent-2411-219: These features are incorporated into the iterative denoising process via RGB-guided residual blocks to modulate the diffusion features.
elements.sent-2411-220: RAW-Diffusion directly predicts the RAW images and learns the precise reconstruction through iterative denoising.
elements.sent-2411-221: Comprehensive experiments on four DSLR camera datasets show the superior performance of RAW-Diffusion over current state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-222: Furthermore, we demonstrate that RAW-Diffusion exhibits remarkable effectiveness, even when trained on a limited dataset with $25$ images or fewer.
elements.sent-2411-223: On the downstream task, RAW-Diffusion enables the generation of realistic RAW datasets, like Cityscapes-RAW and BDD100K-RAW, from large-scale RGB datasets, facilitating the training on new camera sensors with very few training samples from the target domain.

elements.section-2411-012: Training on RGB datasets for object detection on RAW images
elements.sent-2411-246: We analyze if object detection performance on RAW images improves by directly adding RGB samples to the original RAW training set instead of adding converted RAW images.
elements.sent-2411-247: The results of this experiment on NOD Nikon with Cityscapes and BDD100K, respectively, are shown in \cref{tab:exp_results_od_rgb .}
elements.sent-2411-248: Adding RGB training samples does not improve object detection performance as much as our generated RAW dataset.
elements.sent-2411-249: This highlights that the quality and distribution of the training dataset are crucial.
